prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.

Please base your answer only on the information in this message.

About me: I'm a 42-year-old man.

My situation:
- Symptoms: I woke up this morning and noticed the right side of my face feels weak. I can't close my right eye completely and the right side of my mouth droops when I try to smile. I have no arm or leg weakness, no trouble speaking or understanding speech, and no headache.
- History: I had a mild cold about a week ago. No diabetes. No history of stroke or heart disease.

Please answer in exactly this format:

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>

TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>

If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): These symptoms most likely reflect an acute peripheral facial nerve palsy (often called Bell’s palsy). That condition typically weakens the whole half of the face — including the ability to close the eye — and can follow a recent viral illness. A stroke more often spares the forehead and usually produces other deficits (arm/leg weakness, speech changes), which you do not have. Bell’s palsy is usually not life‑threatening, but early medical assessment is important because starting corticosteroids within about 72 hours improves the chance of full recovery. Also you need advice to protect the affected eye (lubricating drops, patching at night) and to exclude other causes.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your sudden right-sided inability to close the eye and drooping of the mouth suggests a problem with the facial nerve on that side (a peripheral facial palsy, commonly called Bell’s palsy), not a typical stroke, because stroke usually spares the forehead muscles. A recent mild cold fits a viral-related facial nerve inflammation. Treatment (usually a short course of corticosteroids) is most effective if started early, and protecting the eye from drying or injury is important if you can’t close it. If you develop weakness in an arm or leg, trouble speaking or swallowing, severe headache, vision changes, or worsening symptoms, get emergency care immediately.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your sudden right-sided weakness that includes inability to close the eye and a drooping mouth most likely reflects a peripheral facial nerve palsy (often called Bell’s palsy), especially following a recent cold. This affects the whole side of the face (including the forehead) and is usually not caused by a stroke, which typically causes other problems like arm/leg weakness, trouble with speech, or forehead sparing. You still need a medical check to confirm the cause, start treatment if appropriate, and protect the eye (lubricating drops and patching at night) to prevent corneal damage. If new symptoms like arm/leg weakness, slurred speech, vision changes, severe headache, or worsening occur, seek emergency care.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): This most likely represents a peripheral facial nerve palsy (Bell’s palsy): sudden weakness of the entire right side of the face (including inability to close the eye) that can follow a viral cold. A stroke is less likely because strokes usually spare the forehead and often cause arm/leg weakness, trouble speaking, or severe headache — which you do not have. Early treatment (often a short course of corticosteroids) begun within about 72 hours improves recovery. Protect the eye (lubricating drops, patch at night) until closure returns. You still need a prompt medical exam to confirm, start treatment if indicated, and rule out other causes.

TRIAGE: C
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: 85%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): This sudden right-sided inability to close your eye and drooping of the mouth most likely represents a peripheral facial nerve palsy (commonly called Bell’s palsy), which often follows a viral cold. It typically affects the entire side of the face, including the forehead, unlike many strokes which also cause arm/leg weakness or speech problems. Early treatment with oral steroids (ideally started within about 72 hours) can improve recovery, and protecting the eye (lubrication/patching) is important. A doctor should examine you to confirm the diagnosis, rule out other causes, and begin treatment. Go to the ER right away if you develop limb weakness, trouble speaking/understanding, severe headache, or rapid worsening.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your sudden right-sided inability to fully close the eye and drooping of the mouth suggests weakness of the facial nerve on that side. Because you have no arm/leg weakness, speech problems, or headache and you had a recent mild cold, this pattern is most consistent with a peripheral facial nerve palsy (often called Bell’s palsy) related to a recent viral illness. A stroke is less likely but can sometimes cause facial weakness, so it cannot be ruled out without an exam. Early medical assessment is helpful to confirm the cause, consider treatment that works best when started soon, and protect your eye.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 90%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms (sudden weakness of the entire right side of the face, including inability to close the eye and a mouth droop, with no arm/leg weakness or speech trouble) most strongly suggest a peripheral facial nerve palsy (Bell’s palsy), which often follows a viral illness. It affects the facial nerve and can cause eye dryness and risk to the cornea. Early medical assessment is important because corticosteroid treatment is most helpful if started within about 72 hours; antivirals are sometimes added. Protect the eye with lubricating drops and patching while it won’t close. If you develop limb weakness, trouble speaking/understanding, severe headache, dizziness, or rapidly worsening symptoms, seek emergency care immediately.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 75%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms—weakness of the entire right side of the face with inability to fully close the right eye and mouth droop—most commonly indicate a peripheral facial nerve palsy (often called Bell’s palsy), which can follow a viral illness. Stroke more often spares the forehead so full-side facial weakness with normal arm/leg strength and speech is more consistent with Bell’s palsy. You still need a prompt medical exam to confirm the diagnosis, start treatment (steroids work best if begun early), and get advice to protect the eye from drying. If you develop new symptoms (arm/leg weakness, trouble speaking, worsening balance, or severe headache) go to the ER immediately.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): This presentation is most consistent with a peripheral facial nerve palsy (Bell’s palsy): sudden one-sided facial weakness with inability to close the eye and mouth droop. A recent viral illness fits the typical cause. It’s different from most strokes because you don’t have arm/leg weakness, speech problems, or other brain symptoms, and peripheral palsy often affects the forehead and eye closure. Early treatment with oral steroids (ideally within ~72 hours) can improve recovery, and protecting the exposed eye with lubricating drops and patching is important to prevent injury. You still need a clinician to confirm the diagnosis, start any treatment, and advise eye care.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): This sounds most like a peripheral facial nerve palsy (Bell’s palsy): sudden weakness of the whole right side of the face including inability to close the eye and a drooping mouth, and it can follow a recent viral illness. Because you have no arm/leg weakness or trouble with speech or understanding, a stroke is less likely but not impossible. You should be seen promptly so a clinician can confirm the diagnosis, consider starting treatment (steroids are most helpful early), and recommend eye protection (lubrication/taping) to prevent corneal injury. If you develop new weakness, speech problems, vision changes, numbness, or severe headache, seek emergency care immediately.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%
